blocks.content: Time and Work questions are based on the relationship between the amount of work done, the time taken, and the efficiency of the worker. If efficiency is constant, then more efficiency means less time for the same job.
blocks.title: Core Idea

blocks.description: This is the base idea behind all time and work questions.
blocks.title: Basic Relationship

blocks.description: If a person completes one job in T days, then one day work rate is 1/T.
blocks.title: Work Rate Formula

blocks.description: When two or more workers work together, their rates are added if they work in the same direction toward the same job.
blocks.formula: Total Work = Sum of Individual Rates
blocks.title: Combined Work Formula

blocks.content: A man-hour is the amount of work done by an average worker in one hour. Time and work questions often behave like man-hour calculations: total work = number of workers × time × efficiency.
blocks.title: Man-Hour Idea

steps: Add or subtract rates depending on the situation
Convert the total rate into time
Check whether the answer is in days, hours, or both
blocks.title: How to Solve Time and Work Questions

rows.right: The total task to be completed
Duration required to complete the task
How fast or slow a worker completes the same task
Work completed in one unit of time

items.detail: Assume total work as 1 if no real value is given.
items.label: Step 1

items.detail: Add all daily works if they work together.
items.label: Step 3

items.detail: Take reciprocal of total work per day to get total time.
items.label: Step 4

blocks.memory_trick: Think of it as: more rate, less time; less rate, more time. If one person finishes in 10 days, daily work is 1/10.
blocks.title: Memory Trick

blocks.tip: Days cannot be added. Only the fraction of work done per day can be combined.
blocks.title: Common Mistake
blocks.wrong: Adding days directly when two workers work together.

rows: One person finishes in T days
Combined work = sum of individual works
If A is twice as efficient as B
blocks.title: Quick Reference Table

blocks.content: For reasoning exams, the cleanest approach is to convert every worker into a fractional daily rate. That makes large questions much easier.
blocks.title: Important Note

blocks.find: Time taken if A and B work together
blocks.formula_used: Total Time = 1 / (Sum of Daily Work Rates)
given: A can complete a work in 10 days
B can complete the same work in 15 days
blocks.title: Basic Example

points: If total work is unknown, assume it as 1
Efficiency and time are inversely related
blocks.title: Quick Revision

blocks.subtitle: The Most Important Concept in Time & Work
blocks.title: Work and Efficiency

blocks.content: Efficiency is the amount of work completed in a unit of time. A more efficient worker completes more work in the same time or the same work in less time.
blocks.title: What is Efficiency?

blocks.description: Efficiency and time are inversely proportional for the same work.
blocks.title: Efficiency Relationship

blocks.content: If A is twice as efficient as B, then A will take half the time taken by B to complete the same work.
blocks.title: Golden Rule

steps: Reverse it to get time ratio
blocks.title: How Efficiency Questions Are Solved

blocks.content: In competitive exams, total work is often assumed as the LCM of individual completion times. This eliminates fractions and makes calculations faster.
blocks.title: Why LCM Method Works

blocks.formula_used: Efficiency Ratio = Inverse of Time Ratio
given: A is twice as efficient as B
B completes a work in 24 days
blocks.title: Efficiency Example

blocks.formula_used: Time = Total Work / Combined Daily Work
blocks.title: LCM Method Example

blocks.correct: Time ratio is always the inverse of efficiency ratio.
blocks.tip: Higher efficiency means less time.
blocks.title: Most Common Trap
blocks.wrong: Using efficiency ratio directly as time ratio.

blocks.content: Whenever completion times are given, first check if the LCM method can simplify the question. It is usually faster than fractions.
blocks.title: Exam Shortcut

blocks.explanation: Efficiency ratio = 3:1. Time ratio = 1:3. Therefore A takes 18/3 = 6 days.
blocks.question: If A is three times as efficient as B and B completes a work in 18 days, how many days will A take?
A and B can complete a work in 12 days and 18 days respectively. Using LCM method, total work assumed is:

points: Efficiency and time are inversely proportional.
Higher efficiency means lower completion time.
Time ratio is the inverse of efficiency ratio.
Combined work = sum of individual work rates.
blocks.title: Quick Revision

blocks.subtitle: The Foundation of Most Competitive Exam Questions
blocks.title: One Man and Two Men Work Problems

blocks.content: If a person completes a job in T days, then the work done by him in one day is 1/T. This is called the work rate.
blocks.title: One Man Work Concept

blocks.description: The most important formula in Time and Work.
blocks.title: One Day Work

blocks.content: When two persons work together, their daily work rates are added because both contribute simultaneously toward the same task.
blocks.title: Two Persons Working Together

blocks.description: Where A and B are days taken individually.
blocks.title: Combined Work

steps: Take reciprocal to find total time
Convert answer into days or hours
blocks.title: Standard Solution Method

items.detail: Write one-day work for each worker.
items.label: Step 1

items.detail: Find total work completed per day.
items.label: Step 3

blocks.correct: Add work rates, not completion times.
blocks.tip: This is one of the most common mistakes in aptitude exams.
blocks.title: Dangerous Mistake
blocks.wrong: 10 days + 15 days = 25 days together

blocks.tip: Combined work is never found using simple average.
blocks.title: Another Trap
blocks.wrong: Taking average of individual times.

blocks.memory_trick: Rate Added Together. Whenever workers join, add their rates.
blocks.title: Memory Trick

blocks.content: If A takes x days and B takes y days, together they take (xy)/(x+y) days.
blocks.title: Important Shortcut

blocks.description: Applicable when exactly two workers work together.
blocks.title: Direct Shortcut Formula

blocks.question: A can complete a work in 15 days. What is A's one-day work?
A and B complete a work in 12 days and 18 days respectively. How many days will they take together?
If A takes 20 days and B takes 30 days, together they take:

points: Add work rates, never add days.
Use LCM method to avoid fractions.
Higher work rate means less completion time.
blocks.title: Quick Revision

subtopics.title: One Man Work and Two Men Working Together

blocks.content: When three or more workers work together, their individual work rates are added. The combined rate determines the total time required.
blocks.title: Core Principle

blocks.description: Combined one-day work of A, B and C.
blocks.title: Three Worker Formula

blocks.description: Applicable to any number of workers.
blocks.formula: Combined Work = Sum of Individual Work Rates
blocks.title: General Formula

steps: Take reciprocal if total work is assumed as 1
Calculate remaining work if workers join or leave
blocks.title: How to Solve Multi-Worker Questions

blocks.content: Many exam questions involve workers joining after some days or leaving before completion. Solve such questions by calculating work completed during each phase separately.
blocks.title: Joining and Leaving Problems

given: B completes work alone in 18 days
blocks.title: Worker Joins Later

blocks.formula_used: Remaining Work = Total Work - Completed Work
blocks.title: Worker Leaves Midway

blocks.tip: Rates can be combined, completion times cannot.
blocks.title: Most Common Mistake
blocks.wrong: Adding total completion times directly.

blocks.correct: Recalculate rate whenever a worker joins or leaves.
blocks.tip: Treat each phase separately.
blocks.title: Joining-Leaving Trap
blocks.wrong: Using the same combined rate throughout the question.

blocks.content: Most difficult Time & Work questions become easy if you divide them into separate phases and calculate work completed in each phase.
blocks.title: Advanced Exam Insight

blocks.question: A, B and C complete a work in 12, 18 and 36 days respectively. Together they finish the work in:
If A does 1/10 work per day and B does 1/15 work per day, their combined work per day is:

blocks.explanation: Work done before joining must be calculated first, then remaining work is found.
blocks.question: A worker joins after some days. What should be done first?

points: Combined work equals sum of individual work rates.
Find remaining work after each phase.
More workers means less completion time.
blocks.title: Quick Revision

subtopics.title: Three or More Workers Working Together

blocks.content: In Time and Work problems, men, women and children often have different efficiencies. Such questions are solved by converting everyone into a common efficiency unit.
blocks.title: Core Concept

blocks.content: Instead of calculating separately, convert all workers into equivalent men, equivalent women or equivalent children and then solve the question.
blocks.title: Equivalent Worker Method

blocks.description: Workers with higher efficiency complete more work in the same time.
blocks.title: Efficiency Principle

blocks.description: Used to convert different worker categories into a common unit.
blocks.title: Equivalent Conversion

steps: Convert all workers into that unit
blocks.title: Standard Solving Method

items.detail: Convert workers into a common unit.
items.label: Step 2

items.detail: Find total time or required workers.
items.label: Step 4

steps: 3 Men do work equivalent to 2 Women
blocks.title: Equivalent Men Example

blocks.tip: Worker count alone does not determine productivity.
blocks.title: Very Common Mistake
blocks.wrong: Comparing number of workers directly.

blocks.correct: Time ratio is inverse of efficiency ratio.
blocks.tip: Always verify whether ratio represents time or efficiency.
blocks.title: Ratio Trap
blocks.wrong: Using time ratio as efficiency ratio.

blocks.content: Whenever men, women and children appear together, first convert them into a single unit before doing any calculation.
blocks.title: Exam Shortcut

blocks.question: If 1 Man = 2 Children, then 24 Children are equivalent to:
Efficiency ratio of Men and Women is 3:2. Twenty-one women are equivalent to how many men?

blocks.explanation: The first step is always conversion into a common efficiency unit.
blocks.question: What should be done first in Men-Women-Children problems?

points: Convert workers into a common unit.
Time ratio is inverse of efficiency ratio.
Worker count alone is meaningless without efficiency.
blocks.title: Quick Revision

blocks.subtitle: Time and Work Applied to Water Flow Problems
blocks.title: Pipes and Cisterns

blocks.content: Pipes and Cisterns is an application of Time and Work. Filling pipes perform positive work while outlet pipes perform negative work.
blocks.title: Core Concept

blocks.content: Treat the tank as the total work and treat water flow per hour as work done per hour.
blocks.title: Golden Rule

blocks.description: If a pipe fills a tank in T hours, then hourly work is 1/T.
blocks.title: Filling Pipe Formula

blocks.description: Outlet pipes remove water and are treated as negative work.
blocks.title: Outlet Pipe Formula

blocks.description: Used when multiple pipes operate simultaneously.
blocks.formula: Net Rate = Sum of Filling Rates - Sum of Emptying Rates
blocks.title: Combined Pipe Formula

steps: Find hourly work of each pipe
Assign negative sign to outlet pipes
blocks.title: How to Solve Pipes and Cisterns

given: A pipe fills a tank in 8 hours
blocks.title: Single Pipe Example

given: Pipe A fills tank in 12 hours
Pipe B fills tank in 18 hours
blocks.title: Two Filling Pipes

blocks.tip: Outlet pipes reduce water and therefore reduce net work.
blocks.title: Most Common Trap
blocks.wrong: Adding leakage pipe rate as positive work.

blocks.tip: Always work with hourly rates.
blocks.title: Calculation Trap
blocks.wrong: Subtracting filling times.

blocks.content: Whenever a leak or outlet appears, immediately assign a negative sign to its rate before calculating.
blocks.title: Shortcut

blocks.question: A pipe fills a tank in 8 hours. Its hourly work is:
A tank is filled by a pipe in 10 hours and emptied by a leak in 15 hours. Net filling time is:

blocks.explanation: Outlet pipes remove water from the tank.
blocks.question: Which pipe contributes negative work?

points: Pipes and Cisterns is based on Time and Work.
Add positive rates and subtract negative rates.
Find time using reciprocal of net rate.
Leakage questions are among the most frequently asked exam problems.
blocks.title: Quick Revision

blocks.subtitle: Payment Distribution Based on Work Done
blocks.title: Work and Wages

blocks.content: Wages are directly proportional to the amount of work done. A person who does more work should receive a larger share of the total wages.
blocks.title: Core Principle

blocks.content: Work Ratio = Wage Ratio. If workers contribute work in the ratio 2:3, their wages must also be divided in the ratio 2:3.
blocks.title: Golden Rule

blocks.description: Payment is proportional to contribution.
blocks.title: Basic Wage Formula

blocks.description: Used to calculate each worker's share.
blocks.formula: Individual Wage = (Individual Work / Total Work) × Total Wages
blocks.title: Wage Distribution

steps: Determine work done by each worker
Convert work ratio into wage ratio
blocks.title: How to Solve Work and Wage Problems

given: A is twice as efficient as B
blocks.title: Different Efficiency Example

given: A, B and C work in ratio 2:3:5
blocks.title: Three Workers Example

blocks.content: When efficiencies differ, first calculate effective work done using Work = Efficiency × Time, then distribute wages according to work ratio.
blocks.title: Efficiency Based Wage Questions

blocks.content: Never distribute wages according to time alone unless efficiencies are equal.
blocks.title: Important Shortcut

blocks.tip: Work depends on both time and efficiency.
blocks.title: Most Common Mistake
blocks.wrong: Dividing wages according to working days only.

blocks.tip: Work = Efficiency × Time.
blocks.title: Ratio Trap
blocks.wrong: Using efficiency ratio directly as wage ratio.

blocks.question: Two workers contribute work in the ratio 2:3. Total wages are ₹2500. What is the share of the second worker?

blocks.explanation: Wages are proportional to work done.
blocks.question: If work ratio is 4:5, wage ratio will be:
When efficiencies differ, wages should be divided according to:

points: Wages are proportional to work done.
If efficiencies differ, calculate effective work first.
Do not divide wages according to time alone.
Ratio method solves most exam questions quickly.
blocks.title: Quick Revision

blocks.content: Most Time and Work questions can be solved in less than 30 seconds if the correct shortcut is identified.
blocks.title: Why Shortcuts Matter

blocks.description: If A and B individually complete a work in A and B days.

blocks.description: Higher efficiency means less completion time.

blocks.title: Efficiency Shortcut

blocks.content: Assume total work as the LCM of completion times. This removes fractions and makes calculations significantly easier.
blocks.title: LCM Master Method

blocks.content: For alternate-day questions, calculate work completed in one full cycle first, then find the remaining work.
blocks.title: Alternate Day Shortcut

steps: Calculate work done in one cycle
blocks.title: Alternate Day Strategy

steps: A completes remaining work in 1/3 day
blocks.title: Alternate Day Example

blocks.content: When workers are replaced, calculate work completed before replacement and then continue with the new worker's rate.
blocks.title: Worker Replacement Shortcut

blocks.tip: Completion times are never added.
blocks.title: Trap 1
blocks.wrong: Adding completion times.

blocks.correct: Convert efficiency into work rate first.
blocks.tip: Efficiency controls output.
blocks.title: Trap 2
blocks.wrong: Ignoring efficiency ratio.

blocks.tip: Every phase has its own rate.
blocks.title: Trap 3
blocks.wrong: Using the same rate after a worker leaves.

blocks.tip: Outlet pipes reduce work.
blocks.title: Trap 4
blocks.wrong: Treating leakage pipe as positive work.

blocks.content: In most competitive exams, the LCM method is the fastest and safest technique.

blocks.content: Remember only five things: One Day Work, LCM Method, Combined Work Rate, Inverse Efficiency-Time Relationship, and Remaining Work Concept. Most Time & Work questions are solved using these ideas.
